name: Triangle inequality for normed vector spaces
principles: Euclidean geometry: third side bounded by the sum of the other two
Cauchy-Schwarz as the proof step in inner-product spaces
conventions: ||.|| is any norm on a real or complex vector space V
extends to L^p norms (1 <= p <= infinity) via the Minkowski inequality
metric-space form: d(x, z) <= d(x, y) + d(y, z) for any metric d
complex modulus: |z + w| <= |z| + |w| (subset of the normed-space form)
expectedLimits: equality holds iff u and v are parallel and point in the same direction (i.e. v = lambda u for some lambda >= 0)
u = 0 or v = 0: equality is trivial (||u + v|| = ||u|| or ||v||, equal to the right-hand side)
anti-parallel u and v: strict inequality; ||u + v|| can be much smaller than ||u|| + ||v||
reverse triangle inequality: |||u|| - ||v||| <= ||u - v||  — useful for Lipschitz continuity arguments
polygon generalisation: ||sum_i u_i|| <= sum_i ||u_i|| for any finite (or absolutely convergent) sum
integral form (Minkowski for L^p): ||f + g||_p <= ||f||_p + ||g||_p for 1 <= p <= infinity
references: Euclid — Elements, Book I, Proposition 20 (geometric origin in Euclidean plane)
Minkowski (1896) — Geometrie der Zahlen (general L^p form)
Rudin — Principles of Mathematical Analysis, ch.4
Conway — A Course in Functional Analysis, ch.1
